elements.sent-2411-001: However, most current VR training applications provide unvaried training to all users and do not consider individual differences.
elements.sent-2411-002: One of these key differences is that individuals can experience different Cognitive Load (CL) levels while doing the same task.
elements.sent-2411-003: CL is the mental effort used in working memory \cite{souchet2022measuring , which can impact learning and performance.}
elements.sent-2411-004: A high CL can hinder learning, while an optimal level---neither too frustrating/hard nor boring/easy---can improve it.
elements.sent-2411-005: A promising solution to consider different cognitive abilities is adapting the training and customizing the content based on the user's CL.
elements.sent-2411-006: Therefore, adaptive training based on the participant's CL can significantly improve training outcomes \cite{10395602, paas2003cognitive .}
elements.sent-2411-007: However, detecting high CL in VR environments poses unique challenges.
elements.sent-2411-008: Unlike static environments, dynamic environments include continuous interaction, shifting audio-visual stimuli, and multitasking, all adding to the variation in cognitive demands and making it difficult to accurately capture and analyze CL.
elements.sent-2411-009: In traditional settings, CL can often be assessed using more controlled, static tasks, but these methods fall short in dynamic scenarios where the cognitive demands are constantly shifting \cite{zander2011towards .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Additionally, subjective methods of measuring CL, such as self-reports, may not be practical in VR applications \cite{gao2024exploring,szczepaniak2024predictive .}
elements.sent-2411-011: A promising solution is using physiological sensors to capture users' cognitive state while they are in the VR.
elements.sent-2411-012: Previous studies have explored CL using various physiological sensors, including heart rate variables, electrodermal activity, electroencephalography, and eye-tracking signals \cite{armougum2019virtual, ahmadi2023cognitive, bodaghi2024multimodal .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Eye-tracking, in particular, offers a non-intrusive and practical means of measuring CL in VR \cite{souchet2022measuring .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Previous studies have shown correlations between eye-tracking metrics and CL, specifically pupil dilation \cite{souchet2022measuring, thomay2023towards , but there is limited research on applying these findings in dynamic VR environments \cite{vulpe2023multimodal .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: A vital step towards developing such adaptive training applications is investigating intelligent tools that detect high CL reliably and non-intrusively.
elements.sent-2411-016: Due to complex and non-linear relationships between eye-tracking metrics and CL, Machine Learning (ML) techniques have been under attention in recent years \cite{szczepaniak2024predictive, miles2024cogload .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Current findings reveal that ML models are useful indicators of CL using eye features, obtaining an accuracy of up to 88\% \cite{shojaeizadeh2019detecting, skaramagkas2021cognitive, gao2024exploring .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Problem Statement While VR offers a helpful tool for training, it often lacks the consideration of individual cognitive differences.
elements.sent-2411-019: We aim to leverage ML techniques to predict user's cognitive load based on eye-tracking data.
elements.sent-2411-020: This approach aims to create a reliable prediction system that can be integrated into VR training applications in real-time to personalize the training based on the user's cognitive load.
elements.sent-2411-021: This paper demonstrates this possibility by determining if eye-tracking features, particularly pupil dilation and fixation duration, can accurately predict a user's self-reported CL (as measured through NASA-TLX).
elements.sent-2411-022: We developed a VR training for cold spray, an advanced manufacturing technology that applies coatings of metallic or non-conductive substances to another surface through gas-powered high-velocity spray \cite{papyrin2006cold .}
elements.sent-2411-023: The virtual environment was developed to simulate a cold spray laboratory \cite{nasri2024designing .}
elements.sent-2411-024: First, we designed a VR tutorial room where participants could get familiar with the cold spray environment and interact with objects they would later use during the study.
elements.sent-2411-025: After completing the tutorial, they teleport to the main VR cold spray lab to begin the assembly/disassembly of the Powder Feeder (PF).
elements.sent-2411-026: For context, a PF is a device used to accurately dispense and control the flow of powdered materials in industrial processes, such as in cold spray.
elements.sent-2411-027: A table next to the PF contains all the necessary virtual tools, such as wrenches and screwdrivers.
elements.sent-2411-028: In front of the user, behind the PF, is a panel that displays the task instructions and buttons to request help, reset, or repeat a step (Fig.~\ref{fig:teaser ).}
elements.sent-2411-029: The disassembly task consists of 12 steps, where the user uses tools to remove the parts of the PF in the correct order and manner.
elements.sent-2411-030: These two tasks are designed to be completed in 15 minutes, although this may vary depending on the participant.
elements.sent-2411-031: The task descriptions are tailored for novices, so participants with varying expertise in the cold spray process should be able to complete them successfully.
elements.sent-2411-032: This study focused on evaluating the assembly and disassembly of the powder feeder, a primary task in the cold spray process (Fig.~\ref{fig:user ).}
elements.sent-2411-033: The virtual environment was developed using the Unity engine (version 2020.03.34f1) \cite{unity2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: We used a VR-3 Varjo headset with a horizontal 115° field of view, 90 Hz refresh rate, and 200 Hz frame rate to capture eye movements, including gaze direction and pupil dilation for each eye.
elements.sent-2411-035: We recruited 22 participants, all students at the same university with varying levels of familiarity with VR, and recruited them via posters and email ads around the campus.
elements.sent-2411-036: Gender identification options included were “woman, man, non-binary, prefer to self-describe, and prefer not to say.” Due to technical issues, 3 were discarded, and we had 19 valid eye-tracking datasets.
elements.sent-2411-038: Our study was approved by the IRB.
elements.sent-2411-039: After they had signed a written informed consent form, we showed an introductory video about cold spray to provide context to participants who did not have prior experience with the process.
elements.sent-2411-040: Then, the participants started the VR training.
elements.sent-2411-041: First, a standard 5-point eye-tracking calibration routine was performed in the VR training.
elements.sent-2411-042: Afterward, participants started a tutorial to get familiar with the VR interactions (e.g., grabbing).
elements.sent-2411-043: Next, participants disassembled the powder feeder consisting of 12 steps.
elements.sent-2411-044: Then, they moved to the assembly module, which consisted of 11 steps.
elements.sent-2411-045: After the VR session, participants completed the short version of the NASA Task Load Index (NASA-TLX) to assess the cognitive load of using VRTA \cite{hart2006nasa .}
elements.sent-2411-046: We also collected demographic information at this point.
elements.sent-2411-047: All the surveys were collected via Qualtrics.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Predictors, target variables and Model Architectures
elements.sent-2411-048: In this study, our main goal was to explore a binary classification to detect cognitive load.
elements.sent-2411-049: We used Python 3.12.0 with sklearn, TensorFlow, Pandas, Matplotlib, and NumPy libraries.
elements.sent-2411-050: In pre-processing, we removed the data before starting the VR training tutorial (e.g., calibration and waiting for the app to load).
elements.sent-2411-051: Then, we used the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) to denoise the signal.
elements.sent-2411-052: Afterward, we normalized the data on a scale of 0 to 1.
elements.sent-2411-053: Previous studies show that pupil dilation is strongly correlated with CL \cite{ahmadi2023cognitive, eckert2021cognitive, lee2023measuring and longer fixation duration can reflect high CL \cite{liu2022assessing,meghanathan2015fixation .}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Hence, we extracted fixation duration and pupil dilation using iMotions Lab software (version 10) \cite{imotions2024 with built-in R Notebooks.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Based on established protocols \cite{salvucci2000identifying , we selected the Velocity-Threshold Identification (I-VT) filter to extract fixations, and the minimum fixation duration was set at 60 Ms. Afterward, we calculated the average pupil dilation of both eyes within each fixation and defined fixation duration and pupil dilation as predictors.}
elements.sent-2411-056: The target variable was the mental demand subsection of the NASA-TLX, which reveals the mental and cognitive effort required for the task.
elements.sent-2411-057: We analyzed the distributions of the NASA-TLX mental workload subsection and found that the population could be evenly split into low and high groups, with scores of 1 to 4 classified as low and 5 to 7 as high.
elements.sent-2411-058: Accordingly, we labeled participants into either a high or low group and assigned each the corresponding target variable.
elements.sent-2411-059: Due to the non-linear nature of the predictors and target variables and high-dimensional data, two classifiers—Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) and Random Forest (RF)—were trained to predict the target variable.
elements.sent-2411-060: We used a sliding window, a common approach in ML \cite{gao2023exploring , with a size of 2000.}
elements.sent-2411-061: The MLP model was designed with five hidden layers.
elements.sent-2411-062: We used the hyperbolic tangent activation function across all hidden layers due to its ability to model complex, non-linear relationships.
elements.sent-2411-063: The model was trained using the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 0.00001, which was selected to ensure stable convergence over 500 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-064: A batch size of 256 was chosen to strike a balance between model convergence speed and predictability.
elements.sent-2411-065: Although dropout layers were considered to mitigate overfitting, the model's performance metrics indicated that the current architecture and settings were sufficient to prevent overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-066: The MLP’s performance was carefully monitored, and the training process was optimized to maintain high precision and recall on both the training and test sets.
elements.sent-2411-067: The RF model was fine-tuned using GridSearchCV, a hyperparameter optimization technique that evaluates all possible combinations of specified parameters to determine the best configuration \cite{aurelien2019hands .}
elements.sent-2411-068: The parameters we tuned included the number of trees, maximum tree depth, minimum samples required to split a node, minimum samples required at a leaf node, the number of features considered for splitting, and whether to use bootstrap sampling.
elements.sent-2411-069: We used 3-fold cross-validation to ensure that the selected parameters resulted in a model that predicts unseen data well.
elements.sent-2411-070: On average, the participants took around $\approx$ 26 minutes to complete the powder feeder module.
elements.sent-2411-071: Despite a relatively small dataset, we obtained strong results.
elements.sent-2411-072: The MLP model, evaluated on the test dataset, achieved an accuracy and precision of 0.84, indicating strong prediction capability.
elements.sent-2411-073: The RF model reached an accuracy of 0.72 and a precision of 0.73, suggesting reasonable performance but with some overfitting compared to the MLP model.
elements.sent-2411-074: More details are depicted in Tabel~\ref{tbl1 .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \label{tbl1} \caption{The results of predicting cognitive load with MLP and RF models using fixation duration and mean of pupil dilation.} \columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{ | m{4em} | m{6em}| m{6em} | m{6em} | m{5em} |} Model Accuracy Precision Recall F1 MLP 0.84 0.84 0.94 0.88 RF 0.72 0.73 0.90 0.81 \end{tabular} }
elements.sent-2411-075: This study investigated the feasibility of predicting cognitive load (CL) in virtual reality (VR) training with complex spatiotemporal tasks to develop a novel adaptive VR training.
elements.sent-2411-076: The Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) model, with an accuracy of 0.84, performed better than the Random Forest (RF), with an accuracy of 0.72.
elements.sent-2411-077: This indicated that the MLP model better predicts new data, making it suitable for adapting to VR training.
elements.sent-2411-078: While achieving high training metrics, the RF model showed a potential overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-079: These findings underscore the complexity of predicting CL in spatiotemporal tasks in VR, where dynamic scenes and interactive tasks provide more challenges.
elements.sent-2411-080: However, several concerns must be addressed before implementing an adaptive training system.
elements.sent-2411-081: Privacy is one the main concerns as eye tracking data can be sensitive and reveal personal information about the user such as age, ethnicity, personality traits, emotional state, and certain measures may even reveal specific mental health conditions \cite{kroger2020does .}
elements.sent-2411-082: Hence, ensuring data security and user privacy is essential.
elements.sent-2411-083: Additionally, while our current models show promising results, they are limited to pupil dilation and fixation duration.
elements.sent-2411-084: Features such as saccade velocity, saccade amplitude, and blink rate could enhance model accuracy and robustness \cite{zhang2017cognitive, abdurrahman2021effects but require more advanced models and computational cost.}
elements.sent-2411-085: This study should be considered preliminary work due to its limitations, which are a low number of participants and a limited number of eye features.
elements.sent-2411-086: In future work, we will explore more complex models, such as Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) \cite{yin2018classification and personalized models, which combine two or more models.}
elements.sent-2411-087: A more complex model could improve accuracy in predicting the user's CL in spatiotemporal tasks in VR training.
elements.sent-2411-088: Our future goal is to develop adaptive VR training that personalizes the training experience in real-time based on the user's cognitive load captured in eye-tracking data.
elements.sent-2411-089: Any opinions, findings, or conclusions expressed in this material are those of the authors and do not reflect the views of the NSF or NCMS.
elements.sent-2411-090: The authors further thank the Kostas Research Institute (KRI) at Northeastern University, Naval Sea Systems Command (NAVSEA), VRC Metal Systems, and all the cold spray experts, students, and developers who participated in the design of the AR and VR training systems.. \bibliographystyle{abbrv-doi.}
